summary:
Cecil Rhodes's vision of a Cape-to-Cairo railway — a continuous British-controlled corridor of territory and infrastructure running the length of Africa from the Cape Colony to Egypt — was one of the grand imperial projects of the late nineteenth century, never fully realized but enormously influential in shaping British expansion in eastern and central Africa. Rhodes, the mining magnate who dominated the diamond and gold industries of southern Africa and served as Prime Minister of the Cape Colony, articulated the project as both a commercial enterprise and a geopolitical program: a contiguous British African empire that would ensure British dominance against German and Belgian colonial rivals.

The railway and telegraph line that were built under Rhodes's direction through Bechuanaland (Botswana), Matabeleland, and Mashonaland (Zimbabwe) were the practical expressions of this vision, and the British South Africa Company that Rhodes created to administer these territories was explicitly designed to extend the corridor northward. The occupation of Rhodesia (now Zimbabwe and Zambia) in the 1890s, the telegraph line connecting Cairo to the Cape by 1892, and the railway's advance toward Lake Tanganyika were steps along this planned axis.

The dream of a continuous British corridor was blocked most tangibly by the Belgian Congo (blocking the western route) and by the [[event:3757|Fashoda Incident]] of 1898, after which the French presence in the upper Nile confirmed that the contiguous Cape-to-Cairo belt could not be achieved. The railway itself advanced in stages — reaching the Victoria Falls by 1905 — but never connected to the Egyptian system. Rhodes died in 1902 without seeing his vision completed. The project remained a symbol of late Victorian imperial ambition, combining genuine commercial calculation with a grandiose belief in the civilizing mission of British colonialism.
